subject: Company Law
module_title: Corporate Decision-Making: Resolutions, Meetings and Board Governance
lesson_title: Board Authority, Delegation and Shareholder-Director Relations

questions.question: Section 158 CA 2014 allocates managerial power in a private limited company. Which statement BEST reflects the effect of s.158?

options.A: The shareholders in general meeting are the supreme governing body and may at any time give binding directions to the board of directors
options.B: The directors manage the business of the company subject to the provisions of the Act and the constitution. Shareholders in general meeting cannot override individual board decisions except by exercising constitutional mechanisms (e.g. altering the articles, removing directors)
options.C: The board of directors derives all its authority from an annual delegation resolution passed at the AGM, which may be revoked at any time by ordinary resolution
options.D: The board and the shareholders in general meeting have co-equal authority over all management decisions, and a deadlock between them is resolved by the chairperson's casting vote

questions.explanation: s.158 CA 2014 vests management of the company's business in the board of directors, not in the general meeting. Shareholders cannot give binding directions to the board in respect of individual management decisions. The general meeting's power over the board is indirect — through constitutional mechanisms such as altering the articles (s.32), removing directors (s.146), or refusing to re-elect them.

questions.question: John Shaw & Sons (Salford) Ltd v Shaw [1935] 2 KB 113 (England, applied in Ireland) is a leading case on the division of power between the board and general meeting. What proposition did Greer LJ establish?

options.A: A company in general meeting may always override individual board decisions by ordinary resolution
options.B: Where the articles vest management in the board, the general meeting cannot interfere with particular acts of management. A company is not bound by a direction to its directors given by the general meeting if the articles vest management authority in the board
options.C: Where shareholders own 100% of the shares, they may individually direct the board's decisions without formal meeting procedure
options.D: The board of directors derives its authority from the general meeting and is accountable to the general meeting for every individual decision

questions.explanation: In John Shaw & Sons v Shaw [1935], Greer LJ stated: 'A company is an entity distinct alike from its shareholders and its directors. Some of its powers may, according to its articles, be exercised by directors, certain other powers may be reserved for the shareholders in general meeting. If powers of management are vested in the directors, they and they alone can exercise these powers.' This authority is central to understanding s.158 CA 2014.

questions.question: Automatic Self-Cleansing Filter Syndicate Co Ltd v Cuninghame [1906] 2 Ch 34 (CA) is a foundational authority on the distribution of corporate power. What key principle does this case establish?

options.A: Directors are agents of the shareholders and must follow shareholder instructions given in general meeting by ordinary resolution
options.B: Where the articles vest management in the board, a simple majority resolution in general meeting cannot compel the board to take a specific management action — constitutional power requires alteration of the articles by special resolution
options.C: The board may be directed by the majority of shareholders acting outside general meeting through a shareholders' agreement
options.D: Once appointed, directors are personally liable for any management decision made without the prior approval of the general meeting

questions.explanation: In Automatic Self-Cleansing Filter v Cuninghame [1906], the Court of Appeal held that where the articles vest management authority in the directors, the majority shareholders in general meeting cannot, by ordinary resolution, compel the board to exercise its powers in a particular way. The only way to override the board (short of removing directors) is to alter the articles by special resolution.

questions.question: Bushell v Faith [1970] AC 1099 HL is a landmark case on weighted voting rights in the context of director removal under s.184 CA 1963 (equivalent to s.146 CA 2014). What device was upheld in that case?

options.A: A pre-emption right that gave the director the right to purchase shares from any shareholder who voted to remove them
options.B: An article giving a director whose removal was proposed three votes per share on the removal resolution — despite the shares otherwise carrying one vote — effectively making removal impossible against the director's wishes
options.C: A shareholders' agreement clause requiring unanimous consent before any director could be removed
options.D: A provision in the articles making directorial appointments irrevocable for a fixed term of 10 years

questions.explanation: In Bushell v Faith [1970] HL, the House of Lords upheld an article giving a director-shareholder three votes per share when a resolution to remove them as director was proposed at a meeting, despite having one vote per share otherwise. This device made removal practically impossible without the director's consent. The HL held this was a valid exercise of the freedom of contract in constructing articles.

questions.question: Under s.146 CA 2014, a director may be removed by ordinary resolution with special notice despite anything in the company's articles or any agreement between the director and the company. What does the phrase 'despite anything in the company's articles' NOT override?

options.A: Weighted voting rights attached to specific shares (like those in Bushell v Faith) that make removal practically impossible even if s.146 is formally observed
options.B: A contractual service agreement between the director and the company — s.146 gives the company the statutory power to remove but does not remove the director's right to sue for damages for wrongful termination of their service contract
options.C: Both A and B — weighted voting rights and contractual claims both survive s.146
options.D: Neither A nor B — s.146 creates an absolute power of removal that overrides all articles provisions and all contractual arrangements

questions.explanation: s.146 CA 2014 preserves both: (1) the validity of weighted voting rights (Bushell v Faith [1970]) that may make removal practically impossible while technically not preventing the vote; and (2) any compensation or damages payable under a service agreement (s.146(5) CA 2014 expressly preserves the director's contractual rights). The statutory power to vote is preserved but practical and contractual protections survive.

questions.question: A director facing removal under s.146 CA 2014 exercises their right under s.146(4) to have written representations circulated to members before the meeting. The company refuses to circulate them, arguing the representations are defamatory. What recourse does the director have?

options.A: The director may apply to court to compel circulation of the representations
options.B: The director has no remedy — if the company claims defamation, the statutory obligation to circulate is suspended
options.C: The director may read the representations aloud at the meeting even if written circulation is refused
options.D: The director may apply to court and, if the court is satisfied the claim of defamation is made in bad faith, may order circulation; alternatively, the director may speak to the representations at the meeting itself

questions.explanation: Under s.146(4) CA 2014, a director facing removal has the right to have their representations circulated and to be heard at the meeting. Where the company refuses on grounds of defamation, the director may apply to court. The court may order circulation if satisfied the claim is abusive. The director always retains the right to speak at the meeting under s.146(4)(b).

questions.question: Clark v Workman [1920] 1 IR 107 (Ireland) is an important authority on directors fettering their discretion. What rule does this case establish?

options.A: Directors may bind themselves contractually as to how they will vote on board resolutions, provided the contract is in writing and for valuable consideration
options.B: Directors may not fetter their future discretion to vote as they think fit on any board resolution. An agreement by a director to vote in a predetermined way on a specific resolution is invalid as a breach of fiduciary duty
options.C: A director who has contracted to vote in a particular way may be compelled by injunction to vote in accordance with the contract
options.D: Directors may fetter their discretion only in respect of decisions not involving a conflict of interest

questions.explanation: In Clark v Workman [1920] 1 IR 107, the Irish court held that a director who agreed to vote in favour of a specific resolution at an upcoming board meeting had acted in breach of their fiduciary duty. Directors are obliged to exercise their judgment independently at each board meeting — they cannot pre-commit their discretion. This reflects the director's fundamental duty to act in the company's interests.

questions.question: The board wishes to delegate its management authority to a managing director. Under s.158 CA 2014 and standard model articles, what is required for valid delegation?

options.A: Shareholder approval by ordinary resolution is required before the board can delegate management authority to any individual director
options.B: Valid delegation requires authority in the articles or the constitution; standard model articles typically permit the board to delegate management to a managing director, with such delegation evidenced in board minutes
options.C: Delegation of management authority to a managing director is prohibited — all management decisions must be taken collectively by the board
options.D: Delegation is automatic once a managing director is appointed — no specific authority in the articles is required

questions.explanation: Under s.158 and the model articles, the board may delegate management functions to a managing director or committees, provided the articles authorise such delegation. The delegation should be evidenced in board minutes identifying the scope of authority delegated. The board remains ultimately responsible and retains the power to revoke or modify delegated authority.

questions.question: The board of a company has four directors. Two directors resign simultaneously due to a dispute, leaving only two directors. The articles require a quorum of three for board meetings. The remaining two directors wish to co-opt replacement directors to restore the board. Under standard CA 2014 model articles, can they do this?

options.A: No — if the board falls below quorum, no board action whatsoever is possible, including co-option; a general meeting must be convened by the shareholders
options.B: Yes — standard articles typically permit a board below quorum to act only for the limited purpose of appointing additional directors to restore quorum; the two remaining directors may co-opt replacements
options.C: The two directors may act only with the prior written consent of the shareholders
options.D: The two directors automatically become shadow directors and must comply with the shadow director disclosure requirements before taking any action

questions.explanation: Standard model articles under CA 2014 typically provide that where the number of directors falls below the quorum required for board meetings, the continuing directors may act only for the purpose of increasing the number of directors to the minimum necessary to form a quorum. This prevents total paralysis while maintaining proper governance.

questions.question: Under the Turquand rule (Royal British Bank v Turquand [1856]), a third party dealing with a company in good faith is entitled to assume that internal procedures have been properly followed. Which of the following scenarios would the Turquand rule protect?

options.A: A third party who entered into a contract with the company knowing that the board had not obtained the required shareholder approval for the transaction
options.B: A third party who entered into a contract with a managing director who had authority under the articles to bind the company in ordinary trading transactions, without knowing that the board had not formally delegated authority in this specific case
options.C: A third party who received a share allotment knowing the director had already been removed from office the day before
options.D: A third party who was an insider (e.g. a director) who relied on an undocumented internal approval

questions.explanation: The Turquand rule protects outsiders who deal in good faith with an officer having ostensible authority, even if internal company procedures were not followed. It does not protect those with actual knowledge of the irregularity (Option A), those with constructive notice of limitations (Option C), or insiders who cannot invoke it (Option D).

questions.question: A company's articles vest management in the board. The majority shareholders (60%) pass a resolution in general meeting purporting to instruct the board not to commence certain litigation against a debtor company. The board wishes to proceed with the litigation. What is the legal position?

options.A: The majority shareholders' resolution is binding — the general meeting is the supreme authority and can direct the board on any management matter
options.B: The board may proceed with the litigation despite the shareholders' resolution — where the articles vest management authority in the board, an ordinary resolution in general meeting cannot direct the board on specific management decisions: John Shaw & Sons v Shaw [1935]
options.C: The board must defer to the majority shareholders' resolution as the litigation decision involves a significant financial commitment affecting all shareholders
options.D: The shareholders may only restrain the litigation by obtaining an injunction from the court — a general meeting resolution has no effect on board management decisions

questions.explanation: Where s.158 and the articles vest management authority in the board, the general meeting cannot direct the board on individual management decisions by ordinary resolution. This is the core holding of John Shaw & Sons v Shaw [1935] and Automatic Self-Cleansing Filter v Cuninghame [1906]. The shareholders' only route to override the board is to amend the articles or remove the directors.

questions.question: What is the 'residual powers' doctrine in the context of Irish company law, and when does it arise?

options.A: The doctrine gives shareholders unlimited powers where the board has acted ultra vires
options.B: Where the articles vest some powers in the board and others in the general meeting, but a particular power is not addressed by the articles, that power falls within the residual powers of the general meeting
options.C: The doctrine gives the CEA residual powers to replace a board of directors where corporate governance has broken down
options.D: Residual powers doctrine refers to the Crown's prerogative rights over the affairs of Irish-incorporated companies

questions.explanation: The residual powers doctrine holds that where the articles allocate certain powers to the board but do not address a particular power, that power is reserved to the general meeting. In Bamford v Bamford [1970] and Irvine v Union Bank of Australia (1877), courts recognised that the general meeting retains powers that have not been expressly delegated to the board.

questions.question: A sole director and 100% shareholder of a private company makes all management decisions unilaterally. The company enters into a major contract without any board resolution being passed. Is the contract binding on the company?

options.A: No — all contracts above a certain value require a board resolution; the absence of board minutes renders the contract unenforceable
options.B: Yes — where the sole shareholder is also the sole director, they constitute both the board and the membership; their act binds the company and the absence of formal board minutes is cured by the Duomatic principle (unanimous shareholder-director consent)
options.C: Only if the contract falls within the ordinary course of business — major contracts always require formal board approval
options.D: No — the company must always pass a formal board resolution even for single-member single-director companies; informal approval has no legal effect

questions.explanation: Where a company is controlled by a sole director-shareholder, their single act constitutes both board decision and shareholder approval simultaneously. The Duomatic principle validates informal unanimous consent. A sole director-shareholder company can validly enter any contract through the sole director's act: Re Duomatic [1969]; Kerr v Conduit [2010].

questions.question: Under s.144 CA 2014, the board may act notwithstanding vacancies in its composition. However, a series of director resignations reduces the board below the minimum number required by the articles. Can the remaining director(s) validly exercise all board powers?

options.A: Yes — s.144 permits any remaining directors to exercise all board powers with no limitation
options.B: No — where the board falls below the minimum number required by the articles (not just quorum), the remaining directors may only act to appoint additional directors to restore the minimum number
options.C: The remaining directors may act for up to 6 months before being required to seek shareholder approval to continue
options.D: No — if the board falls below the minimum required by the articles, all executive power reverts to the shareholders in general meeting who must pass ordinary resolutions for each management decision

questions.explanation: Where the number of directors falls below the minimum required by the constitution (not just quorum), the remaining directors' powers are restricted to appointing further directors to restore the minimum number. Standard articles (CA 2014 model articles) explicitly provide for this limited residual authority, consistent with maintaining governance without paralysis.

questions.question: A board resolution is passed 2-1 at a board meeting. The dissenting director claims the resolution was invalid because they were not given adequate notice of the meeting. What is the general rule on notice for board meetings under CA 2014?

options.A: Board meetings always require 7 days written notice — any shorter notice renders all resolutions void
options.B: The CA 2014 prescribes no specific statutory period for notice of board meetings. The articles govern the notice requirement; in default, reasonable notice must be given. A director who attends without objection is taken to have waived any defect in notice
options.C: Board meetings require the same notice as general meetings — 21 days for special resolutions, 7 days for ordinary resolutions
options.D: Board meetings need no prior notice where all directors are present at the same location — physical presence constitutes constructive notice

questions.explanation: CA 2014 does not prescribe a statutory notice period for board meetings — the articles govern this. In default, reasonable notice is required. A director who attends a meeting without objecting to short notice is deemed to have waived the defect. A director who was not given proper notice and did not attend may be able to challenge resolutions passed in their absence.

questions.question: In the context of s.158 CA 2014, what is the significance of 'entrenchment' provisions in a company's constitution that require shareholder approval for specific board decisions?

options.A: Entrenchment provisions that require shareholder approval for board decisions are void as inconsistent with s.158's vesting of management authority in the board
options.B: Companies may, by their constitution, require shareholder approval for specific categories of management decision — this is a valid constitutional limitation on board authority under s.158 CA 2014, which expressly allows management to be subject to 'the provisions of the constitution'
options.C: Entrenchment provisions are only valid where they were included in the original constitution and cannot be added by later amendment
options.D: Entrenchment provisions are only valid where they require unanimous shareholder approval — anything less than unanimity is ineffective against the board

questions.explanation: s.158 CA 2014 gives management authority to the board subject to the constitution. The constitution may validly require shareholder approval for specific decisions — this is a permitted constitutional limitation. Companies commonly include such provisions in their constitutions (or shareholders' agreements incorporated by reference) for major transactions, capital raises, or acquisitions.

questions.question: A director is appointed as an alternate director by an absent director under an article permitting this. The alternate acts in the name of the appointing director at a board meeting and casts a vote. Under what circumstances is the alternate director's vote valid?

options.A: An alternate director has no right to vote — they may only observe and report back to the appointing director
options.B: An alternate director validly appointed under the articles may attend and vote at board meetings in place of the appointing director, subject to any limitations in the articles or the terms of their appointment
options.C: An alternate director may vote only on routine administrative matters — votes on significant commercial decisions are reserved for the appointed director
options.D: An alternate director's votes are effective only if the appointing director has signed a specific written delegation for each individual resolution

questions.explanation: Where the articles permit the appointment of alternate directors, an alternate validly appointed has the full powers of the director they represent at meetings attended in that director's absence. This includes voting rights. The alternate acts as a temporary director and their votes are as valid as those of any other director, subject to any restrictions in the articles.
